meaning: adj.鏂扮邯鍏冪殑,鍒掓椂浠ｇ殑
examples: The Supreme Court had issued its ruling in this epochal desegregation case a few months before Woodward's lectures.
These epochal figures represented lives hard to imitate, but to be acknowledged as possessing higher authority than mere mortals.
This led to the next epochal shift.
This is also an epochal moment, as we see around North Africa and the Middle East.
An epochal shift has been triggered: "Peak oil" is emerging on the demand side.
